misconceptionSummary: Students learn factorising as a separate trick instead of seeing it as the reverse of expanding.

visuals.ruleText: Factorising is the reverse of expanding
visuals.subText: Expanding opens a bracket, factorising rebuilds it

steps.narration: Factorising is not a brand-new process. It reverses what expansion does.
If expansion turns four bracket x plus two into four x plus eight, then factorising takes four x plus eight back to four bracket x plus two.

lines.text: A common factor is a shared building block
That shared part can be pulled outside the bracket

steps.narration: A common factor is something both terms are built from. Factorising pulls that shared part outside the bracket.
The common factor can include a variable too. Here both terms share five x, so five x can be factored out.

visuals.expression: Factorising reveals the shared structure of the expression
visuals.label: Look for what every term has in common

steps.narration: That is the key idea. Factorising is about spotting shared structure and rewriting the expression more compactly.

misconceptionStrip.text: Common mistake: seeing factorising as unrelated to expanding. The two are inverse processes, so you can check factorising by re-expanding.
misconceptionStrip.audioNarration: If you are unsure whether a factorisation is correct, expand it again and check whether you get back to the original expression.
